body_txt: A character needs to prove their own identity or situation to another character who's not likely to believe them. Maybe they're from the future , some other character reincarnated , or disguising as someone else. Maybe they have to pass a God Test . That's when they say the Trust Password — something that will prove their identity beyond a shadow of a doubt. Trust Passwords come in several forms. They can be a Covert Distress Code (a pre-arranged signal that there's an immediate threat), or Spy Speak (often in sign/countersign form). It could be specialized knowledge of certain facts or events . Or it could be an object that has a special significance (such as an object that's been separated in half , so if you have one half only someone you trust has the other). The speaker may provide it spontaneously to prove their identity, or the skeptical character may ask for it. The trope can be Played for Laughs quite easily — the skeptical character might not believe an otherwise airtight password or the password might be common knowledge such that anyone can easily impersonate the character. Sometimes an illustration of The Power of Language . If it's some kind of medical or scientific test, that's an Impostor-Exposing Test . Closely related is Something Only They Would Say , where instead of a piece of knowledge or a codeword, it's a phrase, action, or nervous tic that is so specific to a single character that it reveals their identity, whether deliberately or not. A common variation that combines the two tropes is to ask for a Trust Password, but regardless of whether the character knows the information, their reaction is the real test—for instance, if they get angry at being forced to prove they're who they say they are, their anger might be the actual proof the questioner is looking for. Similarly, Bluff the Impostor is a subtrope where a character tries to determine whether someone is a fake by subtly referring to something that person should know, and judging their reaction—instead of openly asking them, they just drop the reference and see if the person picks up on it. Not to be confused with Secret Word . Especially not to be confused with Safe Word . (Usually.)
